threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Sends NFT to Caller Instead of Winning Bidder - Free NFT Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, the NFT collateral is transferred to params.msgSender (the caller of isolateLiquidate) instead of loanData.lastBidder (the auction winner). The winning bidder's escrowed funds are consumed to repay the debt, but the NFT goes to whoever calls the function after the auction ends. Any address can call isolateLiquidate—there is no access control restricting it to the winning bidder.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at IsolateLogic.sol:370-377 unconditionally transfers the NFT to params.msgSender. The bidder's bid amount (stored in loanData.bidAmount) is moved from escrow to repay debt via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity. The winning bidder loses their entire bid and receives nothing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) and VaultLogic.erc721TransferOutLiquidity(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) use params.msgSender instead of loanData.lastBidder

preconditions: An isolate loan auction has ended (block.timestamp > bidStartTimestamp + auctionDuration)
loanData.lastBidder != address(0) and loanData.bidAmount > 0
Attacker has a different address than loanData.lastBidder

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: NFT recipient is params.msgSender at line 373/377, not loanData.lastBidder
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker monitors on-chain for auctions past auctionEndTimestamp, then calls isolateLiquidate before the legitimate bidder
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives NFT valued >= bidAmount for free (or only paying totalExtraAmount if bid < debt)

economic_questions: What is the expected NFT value relative to bid amounts in active auctions?
Does the attacker need to pay any extraBorrowAmount if bid fully covers debt?
Can the legitimate bidder front-run the attacker?
validation_requirements: Confirm no access control restricting isolateLiquidate caller to lastBidder
Confirm extraBorrowAmounts[nidx] == 0 when bidAmount >= borrowAmount
Test end-to-end: third party calls isolateLiquidate and receives NFT

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 ETH if bidAmount >= borrowAmount; only extraAmount otherwise
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of the NFT collateral (potentially tens of ETH per NFT)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI when no extra payment required

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None—attacker needs no capital if bid covers debt
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct theft from auction bidders; undermines entire auction mechanism

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of entire bid amount + failure to receive NFT

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Auction duration is 1 day per config; NFT floor prices range from single-digit to hundreds of ETH

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Reentrancy Guard Enables Protocol State Manipulation During Callback
threats.hypothesis_description: flashLoanERC20 in FlashLoan.sol deliberately omits the nonReentrant modifier. During the IFlashLoanReceiver callback, the reentrancy lock is NOT set, allowing the callback to invoke any protocol function (deposit, borrow, withdraw, liquidate, or even nested flash loans). Critically, availableLiquidity is not decremented during the flash loan, so all protocol operations during the callback see inflated liquidity. This causes updateInterestRates to compute artificially low utilization and store incorrect borrow/supply rates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comment in FlashLoan.sol says 'Don't use nonReentrant modifier here'. erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan bypass availableLiquidity tracking entirely. During callback, any call to updateInterestRates sees inflated availableLiquidity, computing lower utilization. The stored rates persist until the next rate update.

proof_of_signal.pattern: flashLoanERC20 uses only whenNotPaused without nonReentrant; erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does not update availableLiquidity

preconditions: Flash loan-enabled ERC20 assets exist in the pool
Pool has sufficient balance to flash loan
Attacker deploys an IFlashLoanReceiver contract that re-enters protocol functions

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updateInterestRates uses assetData.availableLiquidity which is not reduced by flash loan outflow
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan large amount → callback deposits 1 wei to trigger updateInterestRates with inflated liquidity → artificially low rates stored
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker with large existing borrows benefits from reduced interest; nested flash loans amplify manipulation

economic_questions: What is the quantified rate reduction from a max-size flash loan?
Can the attacker persistently manipulate rates across multiple blocks?
Can nested flash loans compound the manipulation?
Can borrow operations during callback access bid escrow funds via inflated availableLiquidity?
validation_requirements: Calculate utilization difference with and without flash loan amount in availableLiquidity
Test re-entry of deposit, borrow, and withdraw during flash loan callback
Verify whether erc20TransferOutLiquidity allows borrowing against bid escrow during callback

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.05 ETH gas for flash loan + callback operations
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Interest rate savings proportional to borrow size and manipulation duration
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Dependent on pool TVL and attacker's borrow position size

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool must have sufficient balance for flash loan
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Anytime; most profitable when pool utilization is high
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systematic undercharging of borrowers, reduced lender yield

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders in affected asset pools
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced yield proportional to rate manipulation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire interest income of the pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Pool WETH utilization ~65%, rate models configured with 5-10% base rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute rate with/without flash-loaned amount in denominator

threats.title: Isolate Redeem Decreases Wrong User's Borrow Balance Creating Phantom Debt and Free Debt Reduction
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateRedeem, erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow is called with params.msgSender instead of the actual borrower (tokenData.owner). When a third party with their own isolate borrows calls redeem for another user's auctioned loan, the caller's borrow balance is reduced while the actual borrower's balance remains unchanged. This creates phantom debt for the borrower (their userScaledIsolateBorrow exceeds their actual loan scaledAmounts) and gives the caller free debt reduction.
threats.investigation_rationale: IsolateLogic.sol line ~263: VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled). Should be tokenData.owner or the original onBehalf account. The loan's scaledAmount is correctly reduced but the wrong user's per-account borrow is decreased.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) uses params.msgSender instead of the loan's borrower

preconditions: An isolate loan is in AUCTION status
Attacker has their own isolate borrows in the same debt group as the auction loan
Attacker's userScaledIsolateBorrow >= amountScaled being reduced

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: params.msgSender used instead of tokenData.owner in borrow decrease
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls isolateRedeem for another user's auctioned NFT, reducing own borrow instead of the actual borrower's
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker pays redeemAmount + bidFine but gets equivalent debt reduction. Original borrower accumulates phantom debt exceeding their actual loan amounts.

economic_questions: Can the attacker profit directly by reducing their own debt this way?
What is the downstream impact of phantom debt on the borrower?
Can phantom debt cause cascading failures in health factor calculations?
Can the borrower ever repay the phantom debt?
validation_requirements: Trace accounting of userScaledIsolateBorrow vs loanData.scaledAmount after exploit
Test whether borrower can repay remaining loans with inflated userScaledIsolateBorrow
Verify that attacker's debt is economically reduced

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Attacker's net benefit is the economic value of reduced debt minus redeemAmount minus bidFine; primary damage is to the borrower's account state

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker must have ERC20 to pay redeemAmount + bidFine

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: The original borrower whose loan is redeemed, plus protocol solvency
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Borrower accumulates unreplayable phantom debt; may face wrongful liquidation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All isolate lending positions in affected groups

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Track userScaledIsolateBorrow deltas for both accounts

threats.title: ERC721 Flash Loan Missing lockerAddr Check Allows Flash-Loaning Locked NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC721 checks tokenData.owner == msgSender and loan status but does NOT check tokenData.lockerAddr. NFTs locked by isolate lending (lockerAddr set to protocol address) or yield managers can be flash-loaned out of the pool. During the callback, the attacker controls the NFT temporarily, potentially claiming airdrops, manipulating delegation, or triggering external protocol interactions that should be restricted while the NFT is locked as collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: FlashLoanLogic.sol validateFlashLoanERC721Basic and the loop in executeFlashLoanERC721 check owner and loan status but never reference tokenData.lockerAddr. VaultLogic.erc721TransferOutOnFlashLoan physically transfers the NFT to the receiver, bypassing the locker guarantee.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop checks tokenData.owner and loanData.loanStatus but never checks tokenData.lockerAddr != address(0)

preconditions: User owns an ERC721 deposited in isolate mode with lockerAddr set (active isolate loan or yield lock)
The ERC721 asset has flash loan enabled
The isolate loan status is ACTIVE (checked in code)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan a locked NFT → during callback, claim airdrops, manipulate yield positions, or interact with external protocols
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value extraction from airdrops or yield manipulations on locked NFTs

economic_questions: What yield managers interact with locked NFTs and could be disrupted?
Are there pending airdrops that could be claimed during the flash loan window?
Can the flash loan callback change external protocol state in ways that break the locker guarantee?
validation_requirements: Confirm lockerAddr is never checked in flash loan flow
Test flash-loaning an NFT with active isolate loan and non-zero lockerAddr
Assess yield manager interactions during flash loan window

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of airdrops or yield manipulation; variable

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When airdrops are pending or yield positions are active
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks locker security guarantees relied upon by yield managers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Yield managers relying on NFT lock guarantees, other protocol users
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of expected airdrop or yield value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All locked NFTs with active yield or isolate positions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Identify all NFTs with lockerAddr != address(0)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm safeTransferFrom physically moves NFT despite lockerAddr

threats.title: Cross-Liquidation Rounding in Debt Repayment Can Create Unliquidatable Positions
threats.hypothesis_description: In _repayUserERC20Debt during cross-margin liquidation, debt amounts are computed as scaledBorrow.rayMul(borrowIndex), then erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow divides back by borrowIndex using rayDiv. Due to rounding in rayMul (round half-up) followed by rayDiv (round half-up), the resulting amountScaled can exceed the original scaledBorrow by 1, causing arithmetic underflow revert in the subtraction groupData.userScaledCrossBorrow[account] -= amountScaled.
threats.investigation_rationale: LiquidationLogic.sol _repayUserERC20Debt computes curDebtRepayAmount = scaledBorrow.rayMul(index) at line ~275. When this full amount is passed to erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow, VaultLogic divides by the same index: amountScaled = curDebtRepayAmount.rayDiv(index). If rayMul rounds up and rayDiv also rounds up, amountScaled > scaledBorrow, causing underflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: curDebtRepayAmount = scaledBorrow.rayMul(borrowIndex), then erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow(curDebtRepayAmount) does rayDiv(borrowIndex), rounding can cause amountScaled > original scaledBorrow

preconditions: User has a cross-margin borrow with specific scaled amount that triggers rounding edge case
Health factor drops below liquidation threshold
Liquidator attempts full group debt repayment

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rayMul(x, idx).rayDiv(idx) may produce x+1 for specific x and idx values
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker deliberately creates borrow positions with scaled amounts that trigger rounding, making their position immune to liquidation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Unliquidatable positions can accumulate bad debt; attacker borrows without repayment risk

economic_questions: What scaled borrow values and borrow indices trigger the rounding edge case?
Is it economically feasible to engineer a position that reliably triggers this?
Does partial repayment also trigger the issue?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test rayMul(x, idx).rayDiv(idx) to find values where result > x
Construct a cross-margin position and attempt liquidation with identified edge case values
Verify whether the revert prevents all liquidation or only specific group liquidation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Collateral sufficient to open a borrow position with targeted scaled amount
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full borrow amount becomes bad debt if position is unliquidatable
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% of borrowed amount if attacker walks away from undercollateralized position

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Position must be created with precise scaled amounts; then wait for price drop
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bad debt accumulation threatens protocol solvency

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lenders in affected pools bear bad debt
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of bad debt across all lenders
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All unbacked debt from unliquidatable positions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: borrowIndex values and scaled amounts triggering rayMul/rayDiv mismatch
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Mathematical proof that edge case exists with current RAY precision

threats.title: Native Token (ETH) Withdrawal Uses Uncapped Amount for Unwrap Causing DoS or Excess WETH Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: In BVault.withdrawERC20 with native token, executeWithdrawERC20 may cap params.amount to userBalance (modifying the memory struct internally), but the original 'amount' variable in withdrawERC20 is unchanged. unwrapNativeTokenInWallet is then called with the original uncapped amount, attempting to pull that amount of WETH from msgSender. This causes reverts for any partial withdrawal or type(uint256).max withdrawals with native ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: BVault.sol line ~68-72: after executeWithdrawERC20 modifies params.amount internally, the outer function still uses the original 'amount'. unwrapNativeTokenInWallet(asset, msgSender, amount) at line ~72 attempts WETH.transferFrom(msgSender, pool, amount) which will revert if amount > actual WETH held by msgSender.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Original 'amount' variable used for unwrapNativeTokenInWallet after executeWithdrawERC20 may have capped params.amount internally

preconditions: User has supplied WETH via native ETH deposit
User attempts withdrawal with amount > actual supply balance or uses type(uint256).max
Asset is the native token (ETH)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: executeWithdrawERC20 caps params.amount to userBalance, but outer scope retains original uncapped amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any native ETH withdrawal where requested amount != actual withdrawal amount causes revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users' ETH becomes locked; withdrawal only works if amount exactly equals userBalance

economic_questions: Does this completely block native ETH withdrawals when using max uint?
Can users work around by withdrawing exact amounts?
Does this affect native ETH paths in other functions (borrow, liquidation)?
validation_requirements: Test withdrawERC20 with native token using amount > userBalance
Verify WETH.transferFrom behavior when amount exceeds balance
Check if receiver parameter divergence from msgSender causes additional issues

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - this is a DoS condition
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; causes user fund lockup

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Affects all native ETH withdrawal attempts with imprecise amounts
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks native ETH integration for common withdrawal patterns

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who deposited native ETH and attempt standard withdrawal
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Temporary fund lockup; workaround may exist via WETH withdrawal
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All native ETH deposits in the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: wrappedNativeToken = WETH at known addresses per chain
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare amount before and after executeWithdrawERC20

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Does Not Validate Available Liquidity Enabling Temporary Drain of Bid Escrow Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does not check or decrement assetData.availableLiquidity, and transfers from the pool's total ERC20 balance. If the pool holds bid escrow amounts (totalBidAmout), flash loans can access those funds. During the callback window, bid refunds or other bid-related operations could fail because the actual token balance is temporarily depleted beyond what availableLiquidity tracks.
threats.investigation_rationale: VaultLogic.erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan simply calls safeTransfer without checking availableLiquidity. The pool's balanceOf includes availableLiquidity + totalBidAmout. A flash loan can drain both pools simultaneously. During the callback, if a nested flash loan or reentered operation tries to access bid amounts, it may fail or succeed incorrectly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan uses safeTransfer without checking availableLiquidity or totalBidAmout bounds

preconditions: Pool has active isolate auctions with non-zero totalBidAmout
Flash loan amount > availableLiquidity but <= balanceOf(pool)
Flash loan callback re-enters protocol to trigger bid-related operations

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Flash loan can access up to balanceOf(pool) = availableLiquidity + totalBidAmout
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan drains bid escrow temporarily; during callback, auction operations may malfunction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Could cause bid refund failures or enable state manipulation when bid accounting is temporarily inconsistent

economic_questions: Can bid refunds fail during flash loan callback window?
Does the reentrancy allow auction operations that see depleted bid balance?
Are there any state changes during callback that permanently affect bid accounting?
validation_requirements: Flash loan amount > availableLiquidity and verify transfer succeeds
During callback, attempt isolateAuction or isolateRedeem operations
Check if bid escrow invariant (totalBidAmout <= actual bid funds) is violated

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on whether bid escrow manipulation leads to extractable value

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool must have active auctions with bid amounts
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When totalBidAmout is significant relative to availableLiquidity

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Auction bidders whose bids might be temporarily inaccessible
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential bid refund failure or auction manipulation

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify flash loan can exceed availableLiquidity limit

threats.title: Fee Collection Reads and Zeros accruedFee Before Index Update Causing Overpayment to Treasury
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCollectFeeToTreasury, accruedFee is read and set to 0 BEFORE InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs is called. Then amountToCollect = accruedFee.rayMul(normalizedIncome) uses the post-update supply index. The new fees accrued during updateInterestIndexs are written to assetData.accruedFee AFTER it was zeroed, meaning they're excluded from this collection. However, erc20IncreaseCrossSupply mints supply shares to treasury without actual token transfer, creating shares backed only by interest obligations—a supply inflation that dilutes other depositors if the accounting has any gap.
threats.investigation_rationale: PoolLogic.sol executeCollectFeeToTreasury: line order is accruedFee=0 → updateInterestIndexs → getNormalizedSupplyIncome → erc20IncreaseCrossSupply. The mint-without-transfer pattern increases totalScaledCrossSupply without increasing availableLiquidity, relying on the assumption that accrued interest perfectly backs the minted shares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assetData.accruedFee = 0 before updateInterestIndexs; then erc20IncreaseCrossSupply mints shares without token transfer

preconditions: Pool admin calls collectFeeToTreasury
Asset has non-zero accruedFee and pending interest updates

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Supply shares minted to treasury without corresponding liquidity increase; relies on debt backing
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If supply/debt accounting has any rounding gap, treasury shares dilute depositors
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Minor dilution of existing depositor shares per collection event

economic_questions: Does the minted supply amount exactly equal the fee-backed portion of outstanding debt?
Are there rounding errors that accumulate across multiple fee collections?
Can a malicious admin repeatedly collect to amplify dilution?
validation_requirements: Compare totalScaledCrossSupply * supplyIndex before and after collection vs actual balance
Verify conservation law: total supply ≈ available liquidity + total debt after collection

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Rounding-level dilution per collection; accumulates over time
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very small per event; significant over protocol lifetime

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After long periods without collection to maximize pending fee amount

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Minor yield reduction from share dilution

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: feeFactor = 2000 (20%), typical pool sizes and interest rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify accounting conservation pre/post fee collection

threats.title: Isolate Auction Interest Accrual Between Bid and Liquidation Can Create Underpaid Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: Interest accrues between the time a bid is placed and when liquidation is executed. The bid amount is fixed at bidding time, but borrowAmount grows with compound interest. In executeIsolateLiquidate, if bidAmount < borrowAmount, the difference (extraBorrowAmounts) must be paid by the caller. However, the caller is NOT the bidder (per ECON-H001). Combined with the NFT theft issue, an attacker can time liquidation to maximize the interval, ensuring extraBorrowAmounts pushes cost onto the legitimate bidder if they try to call liquidate.
threats.investigation_rationale: IsolateLogic.sol executeIsolateLiquidate lines 340-350: borrowAmount = loanData.scaledAmount.rayMul(vars.normalizedIndex) grows over time. loanData.bidAmount is fixed. The gap extraBorrowAmounts = borrowAmount - bidAmount increases with time. The auction duration may be configured to 1 day, during which significant interest can accrue on high-rate loans.

proof_of_signal.pattern: borrowAmount computed with current normalizedIndex; bidAmount is fixed from auction time; gap paid by params.msgSender

preconditions: Active isolate auction with high interest rate group
Significant time between last bid and auction end
Interest accrues enough that bidAmount < borrowAmount at liquidation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: borrowAmount at liquidation = scaledAmount * currentBorrowIndex > bidAmount at bid time
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Bidder locks in bid amount; interest growth creates gap; liquidation caller must cover difference
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: At 100% APY over 1 day auction, interest accrual ≈ 0.27% of principal; for large loans this is material

economic_questions: What is the maximum interest accrual over the auction duration for each rate tier?
Can a bidder bid the minimum amount knowing interest will create a gap?
Combined with ECON-H001, can this be exploited to force costs onto the NFT thief?
validation_requirements: Calculate interest accrual for 1-day auction with high rate group (100% APY)
Verify extraBorrowAmounts computation is correct
Test end-to-end with time progression between bid and liquidation

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 0.1-0.3% of loan value per day in interest gap
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Low per event; systemic impact on auction economics

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High utilization periods with elevated borrow rates

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: auctionDuration = 1 day; borrow rates 5-100% APY; typical loan sizes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare borrowAmount at bid time vs liquidation time

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Allows Recursive Nesting to Amplify Protocol State Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: Since flashLoanERC20 has no nonReentrant modifier, an attacker can nest multiple flash loans within callbacks. Each flash loan transfers tokens out without updating availableLiquidity. Nested flash loans of different assets create a state where multiple assets have inflated availableLiquidity simultaneously. During the innermost callback, protocol operations see inflated liquidity across all flash-loaned assets, potentially enabling cross-asset arbitrage in health factor calculations or interest rate computations.
threats.investigation_rationale: FlashLoan.sol flashLoanERC20 has no nonReentrant guard. FlashLoanLogic.executeFlashLoanERC20 makes external callback. During callback, another flashLoanERC20 can be called for a different asset, each leaving availableLiquidity untouched.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No nonReentrant on flashLoanERC20 allows recursive calls during callback

preconditions: Multiple ERC20 assets with flash loans enabled
Attacker deploys custom IFlashLoanReceiver that nests flash loan calls
Pool has sufficient balance across multiple assets

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each nested flash loan leaves its asset's availableLiquidity inflated
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Nest N flash loans → innermost callback sees N assets with inflated liquidity → perform cross-asset operations at manipulated rates
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Multi-asset rate manipulation compounds effect; cross-collateral health factor checks may pass incorrectly

economic_questions: Can nested flash loans create exploitable cross-asset state?
Does inflated liquidity across multiple assets enable borrowing beyond true capacity?
Can interest rate manipulation across multiple assets compound?
validation_requirements: Test nested flashLoanERC20 calls across WETH, USDT, DAI
Measure rate impact of multi-asset liquidity inflation
Attempt cross-asset borrow operations during nested callback

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.1 ETH gas for complex nested operations
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Amplified version of single-asset rate manipulation

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Multiple pools must have flash-loanable assets
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool utilization is high across multiple assets

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total interest income across all affected pools

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Multiple asset pools; combined TVL and utilization rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate nested flash loans and measure rate divergence

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 Remainder Credited as Supply Without Interest Rate Update for Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCrossLiquidateERC721, when remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 (NFT collateral value exceeds total debt), the excess is credited to the borrower via erc20IncreaseCrossSupply for the debt asset. However, no interest rate update is called for the debt asset after this supply increase—the rate update only accounts for the actualDebtToLiquidate as liquidityAdded, not the remainder that effectively becomes new supply. This causes a mismatch between tracked supply and the rates computed.
threats.investigation_rationale: LiquidationLogic.sol executeCrossLiquidateERC721 lines 190-196: remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 causes erc20IncreaseCrossSupply(debtAssetData, borrower, remainDebtToLiquidate). But updateInterestRates at line 200 only passes actualDebtToLiquidate as liquidityAdded. The remainder supply increase is not reflected in rate calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20IncreaseCrossSupply for remainDebtToLiquidate occurs after updateInterestRates which does not account for it

preconditions: ERC721 cross-liquidation where collateral value significantly exceeds total debt
remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 after repaying all group debts
Non-trivial remainder amount

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updateInterestRates uses actualDebtToLiquidate but not remainDebtToLiquidate
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Repeated ERC721 liquidations with large remainders accumulate supply without rate adjustment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Interest rates become stale relative to actual supply/demand, benefiting borrowers

economic_questions: How large can remainDebtToLiquidate be relative to total supply?
Does the rate mismatch compound across multiple liquidation events?
Does the next operation on the asset correct the rates?
validation_requirements: Calculate typical remainder amounts for ERC721 liquidations
Compare rates before and after liquidation with large remainder
Verify whether next deposit/borrow corrects the rate discrepancy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Capital to cover debt for ERC721 liquidation
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Minor rate inaccuracy per liquidation event

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient to cover ERC721 liquidation debt
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When NFT collateral significantly exceeds debt value

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Interest income deviation for the affected period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: NFT prices vs typical borrow amounts; liquidationBonus = 1000 (10%)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute rate deviation from missing remainder in liquidityAdded
